name: Chris Mearns
title: Founder of LIV3
longBio:
Chris Mearns is the founder of LIV3 Health and a researcher focused on building a unifying framework for metabolic disease.

His work begins with a simple observation. Chronic conditions rarely exist in isolation. Obesity, insulin resistance, fatty liver, cognitive decline, and hormonal dysfunction tend to cluster together. That pattern suggests a shared upstream constraint rather than independent failures.
Chris’s research focuses on cellular energy as that constraint.

Drawing on mitochondrial biology, the cell danger response, and recent work on fructose metabolism, he explores the idea that metabolism is not just about fuel intake, but about how energy is regulated at the cellular level. In this model, glucose functions primarily as fuel, while fructose acts as a regulator that can shift cells into a low-energy, defensive state when chronically activated.

This perspective reframes metabolic dysfunction as a persistence of an adaptive survival pathway rather than a failure of willpower or simple caloric imbalance.

His current work focuses on the fructokinase pathway, where early pharmaceutical research has shown improvements in liver fat and insulin resistance when inhibited. Chris is building on this emerging science to explore whether similar effects can be achieved safely through nutrition and supplementation.

He continues to develop the model publicly, inviting critique and encouraging others to test, challenge, and refine the idea.
shortBio:
Chris Mearns is a metabolic health researcher and founder focused on a missing layer beneath modern metabolic models.

Rather than debating calories, insulin, or hormones in isolation, he studies a shared early energy state that appears across chronic diseases, a defensive, low-energy posture that forms easily under modern conditions and becomes progressively harder to exit. His work helps explain why existing models are directionally correct, why popular therapies often help but plateau, and why relapse is so common once interventions stop.
